exercises.question: What are some of the differences between dedicated hosts and dedicated instances?
exercises.answer:
In dedicated hosts you have per host billing, you have more visibility (sockets, cores, ...) and you can control where instance will be placed.
In dedicated instances the billing is per instance but you can't control placement and you don't have visibility of sockets, cores, ...

exercises.question: For what use cases, EC2 dedicated hosts are useful for?
exercises.answer:
* Compliance needs
* When the software license is complex (Bring Your Own License) and doesn't support cloud or multi-tenants
* Regulatory requirements

exercises.question: What are Security Groups?
exercises.answer:
"A security group acts as a virtual firewall that controls the traffic for one or more instances"
More on this subject [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-security-groups.html)

exercises.question: True or False? Security groups only contain deny rules
exercises.answer: False. Security groups only contain allow rules.

exercises.question: True or False? One security group can be attached to multiple instances
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: True or False? Security groups are not locked down to a region and VPC (meaning you don't have to create a new one when switching regions)
exercises.answer: False. They are locked down to regions and VPC.

exercises.question: True or False? By default, when using security groups, all inbound traffic to an EC2 instance is blocked and all outbound traffic is allowed
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: What is the advantage of referencing security groups from a given security group?
exercises.answer:
Imagine you have an instance referencing two security groups, allowing to get inbound traffic from them.
Now imagine you have two instances, each using one of the security groups referenced in the instance we've just mentioned. This means you can get traffic from these two instances because they use security groups which referenced in the instance mentioned at the beginning. No need to use IPs.

exercises.question: What can you attach to an EC2 instance in order to store data?
exercises.answer: EBS

exercises.question: What EC2 reserved instance types are there?
exercises.answer:
Standard RI - most significant discount + suited for steady-state usage
Convertible RI - discount + change attribute of RI + suited for steady-state usage
Scheduled RI - launch within time windows you reserve

Learn more about EC2 RI [here](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/pricing/reserved-instances)

exercises.question: For how long can reserved instances be reserved?
exercises.answer: 1 or 3 years.

exercises.question: What allows you to control inbound and outbound instance traffic?
exercises.answer: Security Groups

exercises.question: What bootstrapping means and how to use it in AWS EC2?
exercises.answer:
Bootstrapping is about launching commands when a machine starts for the first time.
In AWS EC2 this is done using the EC2 user data script.

exercises.question: You get time out when trying reach your application which runs on an EC2 instance. Specify one reason why it would possibly happen
exercises.answer: Security group isn't configured properly.

exercises.question: What is the AWS Instance Connect?
exercises.answer: [AWS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/Connect-using-EC2-Instance-Connect.html): "Amazon EC2 Instance Connect provides a simple and secure way to connect to your Linux instances using Secure Shell (SSH)."

exercises.question: You try to run EC2 commands in an EC2 instance you've just created but it fails due to missing credentials. What would you do?
exercises.answer:
DO NOT configure AWS credentials on the instance (this means anyone else in your account would be able to use and see your credentials).
The best practice is to attach an IAM role with sufficient permissions (like `IAMReadOnlyAccess`)

exercises.question: True or False? Cancelling a Spot instance request terminates the instance
exercises.answer:
False. When you cancel a Spot instance request, you are not terminating the instances created by it.
To terminate such instances, you must cancel the Spot instance request first.

exercises.question: What are Spot Fleets?
exercises.answer: Set of Spot instances and if you would like, also on-demand instances.

exercises.question: What strategies are there to allocate Spot instances?
exercises.answer:
* lowestPrice: launch instances from the pool that has the lowest price
* diversified: distributed across all pools
* capacityOptimized: optimized based on the number of instances

exercises.question: From networking perspective, what do you get by default when running an EC2 instance?
exercises.answer: A private IP and a public IP.

exercises.question: Explain EC2 hibernate
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/Hibernate.html: "Hibernation saves the contents from the instance memory (RAM) to your Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) root volume."

exercises.question: True or False? Using EC2 hibernate option results in having faster instance boot
exercises.answer: True. This is because the operating system isn't restarted or stopped.

exercises.question: What are some use cases for using EC2 hibernate option?
exercises.answer:
* Save RAM state
* Service with long time initialization
* Keep long-running processes

exercises.question: What are some limitations of EC2 hibernate option?
exercises.answer:
* Instance RAM size is limited
* Root volume must be encrypted EBS
* Hibernation time is limited
* Doesn't supports all instances types
* No support for bare metal. Only On-Demand and Reserved instances
* Doesn't supports all AMIs

exercises.question: Explain what is EC2 Nitro
exercises.answer:
* Next generation EC2 instances using new virtualization technology
* Better EBS: 64,000 EBS IOPS
* Better networking: HPC, IPv6
* Better security

exercises.question: What CPU customization is available with EC2?
exercises.answer:
* Modifying number of CPU cores (useful for high RAM and low CPU applications)
* Modifying number of threads per cure (useful for HPC workloads)

exercises.question: Explain EC2 Capacity Reservations
exercises.answer:
* Allows you to ensure you have EC2 capacity when you need it
* Usually combined with Reserved Instances and Saving Plans to achieve cost saving

exercises.question: What is a launch template?
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-launch-templates.html): "You can create a launch template that contains the configuration information to launch an instance. You can use launch templates to store launch parameters so that you do not have to specify them every time you launch an instance"

exercises.question: What is the difference between Launch Configuration and Launch Template?
exercises.answer:
Launch configuration is a legacy form of Launch Template that must be recreated every time you would like to update the configuration.

In addition, launch template has the clear benefits of:
  * Provision both On-Demand and Spot instances
  * supporting multiple versions
  * support creating parameters subsets (used for reuse and inheritance)

exercises.question: Explain Elastic Network Interfaces (ENI)
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-eni.html): "An elastic network interface is a logical networking component in a VPC that represents a virtual network card."

exercises.question: Name at least three attributes the Elastic Network Interfaces (ENI) can include
exercises.answer:
1. One public IPv4 address
2. Mac Address
3. A primary private IPv4 address (from the address range of your VPC)

exercises.question: True or False? ENI are not bound to a specific availability zone
exercises.answer: False. ENI are bound to specific availability zone.

exercises.question: True or False? ENI can be created independently of EC2 instances
exercises.answer: True. They can be attached later on and on the fly (for failover purposes).

exercises.question: What are "Placement Groups"?
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/placement-groups.html): "When you launch a new EC2 instance, the EC2 service attempts to place the instance in such a way that all of your instances are spread out across underlying hardware to minimize correlated failures. You can use placement groups to influence the placement of a group of interdependent instances to meet the needs of your workload."

exercises.question: What Placement Groups strategies are there?
exercises.answer:
* Cluster: places instance close together in an AZ.
* Spread: spreads the instance across the hardware
* Partition: spreads the instances across different partitions (= different sets of hardware/racks) within an AZ

exercises.question:
For each of the following scenarios choose a placement group strategy:

  * High availability is top priority
  * Low latency between instances
  * Instances must be isolated from each other
  * Big Data applications that are partition aware
  * Big Data process that needs to end quickly
exercises.answer:
* High availability is top priority - Spread
  * Low latency between instances - Cluster
  * Instances must be isolated from each other - Spread
  * Big Data applications that are partition aware - Partition
  * Big Data process that needs to end quickly - Cluster

exercises.question: What are the cons and pros of the "Cluster" placement group strategy?
exercises.answer:
Cons: if the hardware fails, all instances fail
Pros: Low latency & high throughput network

exercises.question: What are the cons and pros of the "Spread" placement group strategy?
exercises.answer:
Cons:
  * Current limitation is 7 instances per AZ (per replacement group)
Pros:
  * Maximized high availability (instances on different hardware, span across AZs)

exercises.question: What is VPC?
exercises.answer:
"A logically isolated section of the AWS cloud where you can launch AWS resources in a virtual network that you define"
Read more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc).

exercises.question: True or False? VPC spans multiple regions
exercises.answer: False

exercises.question: True or False? It's possible to have multiple VPCs in one region
exercises.answer: True. As of today, the soft limit is 5.

exercises.question: True or False? Subnets belong to the same VPC, can be in different availability zones
exercises.answer: True. Just to clarify, a single subnet resides entirely in one AZ.

exercises.question: You have noticed your VPC's subnets (which use x.x.x.x/20 CIDR) have 4096 available IP addresses although this CIDR should have 4096 addresses. What is the reason for that?
exercises.answer: AWS reserves 5 IP addresses in each subnet - first 4 and the last one, and so they aren't available for use.

exercises.question: What AWS uses the 5 reserved IP addresses for?
exercises.answer:
x.x.x.0 - network address
x.x.x.1 - VPC router
x.x.x.2 - DNS mapping
x.x.x.3 - future use
x.x.x.255 - broadcast address

exercises.question: What is an Internet Gateway?
exercises.answer:
[AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_Internet_Gateway.html): "component that allows communication between instances in your VPC and the internet"

In addition it's good to know that IGW is:
  * Highly available and redundant
  * Not porivding internet access by its own (you need route tables to be edited)
  * Created separately from VPC

exercises.question: True or False? One or more VPCs can be attached to one Internet Gateway
exercises.answer: False. Only one VPC can be attached to one IGW and vice versa

exercises.question: True or False? NACL allow or deny traffic on the subnet level
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: What is VPC peering?
exercises.answer: [docs.aws](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/what-is-vpc-peering.html): "A VPC peering connection is a networking connection between two VPCs that enables you to route traffic between them using private IPv4 addresses or IPv6 addresses."

exercises.question: True or False? Multiple Internet Gateways can be attached to one VPC
exercises.answer: False. Only one internet gateway can be attached to a single VPC.

exercises.question: You've restarted your EC2 instance and the public IP has changed. How would you deal with it so it won't happen?
exercises.answer: Use Elastic IP which provides you a fixed IP address.

exercises.question: When creating a new VPC, there is an option called "Tenancy". What is it used for?
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/create-vpc.html): `Tenancy` option defines if EC2 instances that you launch into the VPC will run on hardware that's shared with other AWS accounts or on hardware that's dedicated for your use only.

exercises.question: What is an Elastic IP address?
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/elastic-ip-addresses-eip.html): "An Elastic IP address is a static IPv4 address designed for dynamic cloud computing. An Elastic IP address is allocated to your AWS account, and is yours until you release it. By using an Elastic IP address, you can mask the failure of an instance or software by rapidly remapping the address to another instance in your account."

exercises.question: Why would you use an Elastic IP address?
exercises.answer: Let's say you have an instance that you need to shutdown or perform some maintenance on. In that case, what you would want to do is to move the Elastic IP address to another instance that is operational, until you finish to perform the maintenance and then you can move it back to the original instance (or keep it assigned to the second one).

exercises.question: True or False? When stopping and starting an EC2 instance, its public IP changes
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: What are the best practices around Elastic IP?
exercises.answer: The best practice is actually not using them in the first place. It's more common to use a load balancer without a public IP or use a random public IP and register a DNS record to it

exercises.question: True or False? An Elastic IP is free, as long it's not associated with an EC2 instance
exercises.answer: False. An Elastic IP is free of charge as long as **it is ** associated with an EC2 instance. This instance should be running and should have only one Elastic IP.

exercises.question: True or False? Route Tables used to allow or deny traffic from the internet to AWS instances
exercises.answer: False.

exercises.question: Explain Security Groups and Network ACLs
exercises.answer:
* NACL - security layer on the subnet level.
* Security Group - security layer on the instance level.

Read more about it [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-security-groups.html) and [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_SecurityGroups.html)

exercises.question: What is AWS Direct Connect?
exercises.answer: Allows you to connect your corporate network to AWS network.

exercises.question: What would you use if you need a fixed public IP for your EC2 instance?
exercises.answer: Elastic IP

exercises.question: Kratos, your colleague, decided to use a subnet of /27 because he needs 29 IP addresses for EC2 instances. Is Kratos right?
exercises.answer:
No. Since AWS reserves 5 IP addresses for every subnet, Kratos will have 32-5=27 addresses and this is less than what he needs (29).

It's better if Kratos uses a subnet of size /26 but good luck telling him that.

exercises.question: True or False? By default, any new account has a default VPC
exercises.answer: True.

exercises.question: True or False? Default VPC doesn't have internet connectivity and any launched EC2 will only have a private IP assigned
exercises.answer:
False. The default VPC has internet connectivity and any launched EC2 instance gets a public IPv4 address.

In addition, any launched EC2 instance gets a public and private DNS names.

exercises.question:
Which of the following is included with default VPC?

* Internet gateway connected to the default VPC
* A route to main route table that points all traffic to internet gateway
* Default public subnet
* Default /16 IPv4 CIDR block
exercises.answer: All of them :)

exercises.question: Explain what is AWS Lambda
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS Lambda lets you run code without provisioning or managing servers. You pay only for the compute time you consume."

Read more on it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/lambda)

exercises.question: True or False? In AWS Lambda, you are charged as long as a function exists, regardless of whether it's running or not
exercises.answer: False. Charges are being made when the function is executed for the time it takes to execute and compute resources it uses.

exercises.question:
Which of the following set of languages Lambda supports?

- R, Swift, Rust, Kotlin
- Python, Ruby, Go, Kotlin, Bash
- Python, Ruby, PHP, PowerShell, C#, Perl
- Python, Ruby, Go, Node.js, Groovy, C++
- Python, Ruby, Go, Node.js, PowerShell, C#
exercises.answer: - Python, Ruby, Go, Node.js, PowerShell, C#

exercises.question: True or False? Basic lambda permissions allow you only to upload logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: What's one of the issues with the current architecture?
exercises.answer:
Users shouldn't access directly AWS Lambda directly. If you'd to like to expose your Lambda function to users a better approach would be to set up API Gateway endpoint between the users and the Lambda function.

This not only provides enhanced security but also easier access for the user where he can use HTTP or HTTPS for accessing the function.

exercises.question: Specify one or more use cases for using AWS Lambda
exercises.answer:
- Uploading images to S3 and tagging them or inserting information on the images to a database
- Uploading videos to S3 and edit them or add subtitles/captions to them and store the result in S3
- Use SNS and/or SQS to trigger functions based on notifications or messages received from these services.
- Cron Jobs: Use Lambda together with CloudWatch events to schedule tasks/functions periodically.

exercises.question: You run an architecture where you have a Lambda function that uploads images to S3 bucket and stores information on the images in DynamoDB. You would like to expose the function to users so they can invoke it. Your friend Carlos suggests you expose the credentials to the Lambda function. What's your take on that?
exercises.answer:
That's a big no. You shouldn't let users direct access to your Lambda function.

The way to go here and expose the Lambda function to users is to to an API Gateway endpoint.

exercises.question: What is Amazon ECS?
exercises.answer:
[AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/ecs): "Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) is a fully managed container orchestration service. Customers such as Duolingo, Samsung, GE, and Cook Pad use ECS to run their most sensitive and mission critical applications because of its security, reliability, and scalability."

In simpler words, it allows you to launch containers on AWS.
While AWS takes care of starting/stopping containers, you need to provision and maintain the infrastructure where the c...

exercises.question: What one should do in order to make EC2 instance part of an ECS cluster?
exercises.answer: Install ECS agent on it. Some AMIs have built-in configuration for that.

exercises.question: What ECS launch types are there?
exercises.answer:
* EC2 Instance
* AWS Fargate

exercises.question: What is Amazon ECR?
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/ecr): "Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR) is a fully-managed Docker container registry that makes it easy for developers to store, manage, and deploy Docker container images."

exercises.question: What the role "EC2 Instance Profile" is used for in regards to ECS?
exercises.answer:
EC2 Instance Profile used by ECS agent on an EC2 instance to:

  * Make API calls to ECS Service
  * Send logs to CloudWatch from the container
  * Use secrets defined in SSM Parameter Store or Secrets Manager
  * Pull container images from ECR (Registry)

exercises.question: How to share data between containers (some from ECS and some from Fargate)?
exercises.answer: Using EFS is a good way to share data between containers and it works also between different AZs.

exercises.question: What is AWS Fargate?
exercises.answer:
[Amazon Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/fargate): "AWS Fargate is a serverless, pay-as-you-go compute engine that lets you focus on building applications without managing servers. AWS Fargate is compatible with both Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) and Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS)"

In simpler words, AWS Fargate allows you launch containers on AWS without worrying about managing infrastructure. It runs containers based on the CPU and RAM you need.

exercises.question: How AWS Fargate different from AWS ECS?
exercises.answer:
In AWS ECS, you manage the infrastructure - you need to provision and configure the EC2 instances.
While in AWS Fargate, you don't provision or manage the infrastructure, you simply focus on launching Docker containers. You can think of it as the serverless version of AWS ECS.

exercises.question: True or False? Fargate creates an ENI for every task it runs
exercises.answer: True.

exercises.question: Explain what is AWS S3?
exercises.answer:
- S3 is a object storage service which is fast, scalable and durable. S3 enables customers to upload, download or store any file or object that is up to 5 TB in size.
- S3 stands for: Simple Storage Service
- As a user you don't have to worry about filesystems or disk space

exercises.question: What is a bucket?
exercises.answer: An S3 bucket is a resource which is similar to folders in a file system and allows storing objects, which consist of data.

exercises.question: True or False? Buckets are defined globally
exercises.answer: False. They are defined at the region level.

exercises.question: True or False? A bucket name must be globally unique
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: How to rename a bucket in S3?
exercises.answer:
A S3 bucket name is immutable. That means it's not possible to change it, without removing and creating a new bucket.

This is why the process for renaming a bucket is as follows:

* Create a new bucket with the desired name
* Move the data from the old bucket to it
* Delete the old bucket

With the AWS CLI that would be:

```sh
# Create new bucket
aws s3 mb s3://[NEW_BUCKET_NAME]
# Sync the content from the old bucket to the new bucket
$ aws s3 sync s3://[OLD_BUCKET_NAME] s3://[NEW_BUCKET_NAME]...

exercises.question: True or False? The max object size a user can upload in one go, is 5TB
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: Explain "Multi-part upload"
exercises.answer: [Amazon docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/mpuoverview.html): "Multipart upload allows you to upload a single object as a set of parts. Each part is a contiguous portion of the object's data...In general, when your object size reaches 100 MB, you should consider using multipart uploads instead of uploading the object in a single operation."

exercises.question: Explain "Object Versioning"
exercises.answer: When enabled at a bucket level, versioning allows you to upload new version of files, overriding previous version and so be able to easily roll-back and protect your data from being permanently deleted.

exercises.question:
Explain the following:

  - Object Lifecycles
  - Object Sharing
exercises.answer:
* Object Lifecycles - Transfer objects between storage classes based on defined rules of time periods
  * Object Sharing - Share objects via a URL link

exercises.question: Explain Object Durability and Object Availability
exercises.answer:
Object Durability: The percent over a one-year time period that a file will not be lost
Object Availability: The percent over a one-year time period that a file will be accessible

exercises.question: True or False? Every new S3 bucket is public by default
exercises.answer: False. A newly created bucket is private unless it was configured to be public.

exercises.question: What's a presigned URL?
exercises.answer:
Since every newly created bucket is by default private it doesn't allows to share files with users. Even if the person who uploaded them tries to view them, it gets denied.

A presigned URL is a way to bypass that and allow sharing the files with users by including the credentials (token) as part of the URL. It can be done for limited time.

exercises.question: What security measures have you taken in context of S3?
exercises.answer:
* Don't make a bucket public.
	* Enable encryption if it's disabled.
    * Define an access policy

exercises.question: What encryption types supported by S3?
exercises.answer:
* SSE-S3
* SSE-KMS
* SSE-C

exercises.question: Describe shortly how SSE-S3 (AES) encryption works
exercises.answer:
1. You upload a file to S3 using HTTP (or HTTPS) and header
2. S3 uses the managed data key to encrypt it
3. S3 stores the encrypted object in the bucket

exercises.question: True or False? In case of SSE-S3 (AES-256) encryption, you manage the key
exercises.answer: False. S3 manages the key and uses AES-256 algorithm for the encryption.

exercises.question: Who or what manages the keys in the case of SSE-KMS encryption?
exercises.answer: The KMS service.

exercises.question: Why would someone choose to use SSE-KMS instead of SSE-S3?
exercises.answer: SS3-KMS provides control over who has access to the keys and you can also enabled audit trail.

exercises.question: True or False? In case of SSE-C encryption, both S3 and you manage the keys
exercises.answer: False. You manage the keys. It's customer provided keys.

exercises.question: True or False? In case of SSE-C HTTPS must be used and encryption key must be provided in headers for every HTTP request
exercises.answer: True.

exercises.question: Describe shortly how SSE-C encryption works
exercises.answer:
1. User uploads a file to S3 using HTTPS while providing data key in the header
2. AWS S3 performs the encryption using the provided data key and encrypted object is stored in the bucket

If a user would like to get the object, the same data key would have to be provided.

exercises.question:
With which string an header starts?

* x-zmz
* x-amz
* x-ama
exercises.answer: x-amz

exercises.question: What is a storage class? What storage classes are there?
exercises.answer:
Each object has a storage class assigned to, affecting its availability and durability. This also has effect on costs.
Storage classes offered today:
  * Standard:
    * Used for general, all-purpose storage (mostly storage that needs to be accessed frequently)
    * The most expensive storage class
    * 11x9% durability
    * 2x9% availability
    * Default storage class

  * Standard-IA (Infrequent Access)
    * Long lived, infrequently accessed data but must be available the moment it's bein...

exercises.question:
A customer would like to move data which is rarely accessed from standard storage class to the most cheapest class there is. Which storage class should be used?

  * One Zone-IA
  * Glacier Deep Archive
  * Intelligent-Tiering
exercises.answer: Glacier Deep Archive

exercises.question: What Glacier retrieval options are available for the user?
exercises.answer: Expedited, Standard and Bulk

exercises.question: True or False? Each AWS account can store up to 500 PetaByte of data. Any additional storage will cost double
exercises.answer: False. Unlimited capacity.

exercises.question: Explain what is Storage Gateway
exercises.answer:
"AWS Storage Gateway is a hybrid cloud storage service that gives you on-premises access to virtually unlimited cloud storage".
More on Storage Gateway [here](https://aws.amazon.com/storagegateway)

exercises.question:
Explain the following Storage Gateway deployments types

  * File Gateway
  * Volume Gateway
  * Tape Gateway
exercises.answer: Explained in detail [here](https://aws.amazon.com/storagegateway/faqs)

exercises.question: What is the difference between stored volumes and cached volumes?
exercises.answer:
Stored Volumes - Data is located at customer's data center and periodically backed up to AWS
Cached Volumes - Data is stored in AWS cloud and cached at customer's data center for quick access

exercises.question: What is "Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration"?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration enables fast, easy, and secure transfers of files over long distances between your client and an S3 bucket"

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/transfer-acceleration.html)

exercises.question: Explain data consistency
exercises.answer: S3 Data Consistency provides strong read-after-write consistency for PUT and DELETE requests of objects in the S3 bucket in all AWS Regions. S3 always return latest file version.

exercises.question: Can you host dynamic websites on S3? What about static websites?
exercises.answer: No. S3 support only statis hosts. On a static website, individual webpages include static content. They might also contain client-side scripts. By contrast, a dynamic website relies on server-side processing, including server-side scripts such as PHP, JSP, or ASP.NET. Amazon S3 does not support server-side scripting.

exercises.question: In regards to disaster recovery, what is RTO and RPO?
exercises.answer:
RTO - The maximum acceptable length of time that your application can be offline.

RPO - The maximum acceptable length of time during which data might be lost from your application due to an incident.

exercises.question: What types of disaster recovery techniques AWS supports?
exercises.answer:
* The Cold Method - Periodically backups and sending the backups off-site
* Pilot Light - Data is mirrored to an environment which is always running
* Warm Standby - Running scaled down version of production environment
* Multi-site - Duplicated environment that is always running

exercises.question: Which disaster recovery option has the highest downtime and which has the lowest?
exercises.answer:
Lowest - Multi-site
Highest - The cold method

exercises.question: Explain what is CloudFront
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon CloudFront is a fast content delivery network (CDN) service that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to customers globally with low latency, high transfer speeds, all within a developer-friendly environment."

More on CloudFront [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront)

exercises.question: True or False?. Objects are cached for the life of TTL
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: What is AWS Snowball?
exercises.answer: A transport solution which was designed for transferring large amounts of data (petabyte-scale) into and out the AWS cloud.

exercises.question: How can a company ensure their web application continues to operate if it becomes unavailable in its current single region?
exercises.answer: Deploy the application in multiple Regions. Use Amazon Route 53 DNS health checks to route traffic to a healthy Region

exercises.question: What is ELB (Elastic Load Balancing)?
exercises.answer: [AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing): "Elastic Load Balancing automatically distributes incoming application traffic across multiple targets, such as Amazon EC2 instances, containers, IP addresses, and Lambda functions."

exercises.question: True or False? Elastic Load Balancer is a managed resource (= AWS takes care of it)
exercises.answer: True. AWS responsible for making sure ELB is operational and takes care of lifecycle operations like upgrades, maintenance and high availability.

exercises.question: What types of AWS load balancers are there?
exercises.answer:
* Classic Load Balancer (CLB): Mainly for TCP (layer 4) and HTTP, HTTPS (layer 7)
* Application Load Balancer (ALB): Mainly for HTTP, HTTPS and WebSocket
* Network Load Balancer (NLB): Mainly for TCP, TLS and UDP
* Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB): Mainly for layer 3 operations (IP protocol)

exercises.question: Which load balancer would you use for services which use HTTP or HTTPS traffic?
exercises.answer: Application Load Balancer (ALB).

exercises.question: What are some use cases for using Gateway Load Balancer?
exercises.answer:
* Intrusion Detection
* Firewall
* Payload manipulation

exercises.question: Explain "health checks" in the context of AWS ELB
exercises.answer:
Health checks used by ELB to check whether EC2 instance(s) are properly working.
If health checks fail, ELB knows to not forward traffic to that specific EC2 instance where the health checks failed.

exercises.question: True or False? AWS ELB health checks are done on a port and a route
exercises.answer:
True.

For example, port `2017` and endpoint `/health`.

exercises.question: What types of load balancers are supported in EC2 and what are they used for?
exercises.answer:
* Application LB - layer 7 traffic
* Network LB - ultra-high performances or static IP address (layer 4)
* Classic LB - low costs, good for test or dev environments (retired by August 15, 2022)
* Gateway LB - transparent network gateway and and distributes traffic such as firewalls, intrusion detection and prevention systems, and deep packet inspection systems. (layer 3)

exercises.question: Which type of AWS load balancer is used in the following drawing?
exercises.answer: Application Load Balancer (routing based on different endpoints + HTTP is used).

exercises.question: What are possible target groups for ALB (Application Load Balancer)?
exercises.answer:
* EC2 tasks
* ECS instances
* Lambda functions
* Private IP Addresses

exercises.question: True or False? ALB can route only to a single route group
exercises.answer: False. ALB can route to multiple target groups.

exercises.question: If you wanted to analyze network traffic, you would use the `____ load balancer`
exercises.answer: Gateway Load Balancer

exercises.question: Who has better latency? Application Load Balancer or Network Load Balancer?
exercises.answer: Network Load Balancer (~100 ms) as ALB has a latency of ~400 ms

exercises.question: True or False? Network load balancer has one static IP per availability zone
exercises.answer: True.

exercises.question: What are the supported target groups for network load balancer?
exercises.answer:
* EC2 instance
* IP addresses
* Application Load Balancer

exercises.question: What are the supported target groups for gateway load balancer?
exercises.answer:
* EC2 instance
* IP addresses (must be private IPs)

exercises.question: Name one use case for using application load balancer as a target group for network load balancer
exercises.answer: You might want to have a fixed IP address (NLB) and then forward HTTP traffic based on path, query, ... which is then done by ALB

exercises.question: What are some use cases for using Network Load Balancer?
exercises.answer:
* TCP, UDP traffic
* Extreme performance

exercises.question: True or False? Network load balancers operate in layer 4
exercises.answer: True. They forward TCP, UDP traffic.

exercises.question: True or False? It's possible to enable sticky session for network load balancer so the same client is always redirected to the same instance
exercises.answer: False. This is only supported in Classic Load Balancer and Application Load Balancer.

exercises.question: Explain Cross Zone Load Balancing
exercises.answer: With cross zone load balancing, traffic distributed evenly across all (registered) instances in all the availability zones.

exercises.question: True or False? For network load balancer, cross zone load balancing is always on and can't be disabled
exercises.answer: False. It's disabled by default

exercises.question: True or False? In regards to cross zone load balancing, AWS charges you for inter AZ data in network load balancer but no in application load balancer
exercises.answer: True. It charges for inter AZ data in network load balancer, but not in application load balancer

exercises.question: True or False? Both ALB and NLB support multiple listeners with multiple SSL certificates
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: Explain Deregistration Delay (or Connection Draining) in regards to ELB
exercises.answer: The period of time or process of "draining" instances from requests/traffic (basically let it complete all active connections but don't start new ones) so it can be de-registered eventually and ELB won't send requests/traffic to it anymore.

exercises.question: At what network level/layer a Network Load Balancer operates?
exercises.answer: Layer 4

exercises.question: True or False? With ALB (Application Load Balancer) it's possible to do routing based on query string and/or headers
exercises.answer: True.

exercises.question: True or False? For application load balancer, cross zone load balancing is always on and can't be disabled
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: Explain Auto Scaling Group
exercises.answer: [Amazon Docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/AutoScalingGroup.html): "An Auto Scaling group contains a collection of Amazon EC2 instances that are treated as a logical grouping for the purposes of automatic scaling and management. An Auto Scaling group also enables you to use Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling features such as health check replacements and scaling policies"

exercises.question: You have two instance running as part of ASG. You change the desired capacity to 1. What will be the outcome of this change?
exercises.answer: One of the instances will be terminated.

exercises.question: How can you customize the trigger for the scaling in/out of an auto scaling group?
exercises.answer: One way is to use CloudWatch alarms where an alarm will monitor a metric and based on a certain value (or range) you can choose to scale-in or scale-out the ASG.

exercises.question: What are some metrics/rules used for auto scaling
exercises.answer:
* Network In/Out
* Number of requests on ELB per instance
* Average CPU, RAM usage

exercises.question: What is dynamic Scaling policy in regards to Auto Scaling Groups?
exercises.answer:
A policy in which scaling will occur automatically based on different metrics.

There are 3 types:

1. Target Tracking Scaling: scale when the baseline changes (e.g. CPU is over 60%)
2. Step Scaling: more granular scaling where you can choose different actions for different metrics values (e.g. when CPU less than 20%, remove one instance. When CPU is over 40%, add 3 instances)
3. Scheduled Actions: set in advance scaling for specific period of time (e.g. add instances on Monday between 10:00 am ...

exercises.question: What is a predictive scaling policy in regards to Auto Scaling Groups?
exercises.answer: Scale by analyzing historical load and schedule scaling based on forecast load.

exercises.question: Explain scaling cooldowns in regards to Auto Scaling Groups
exercises.answer: During a scaling cooldown, ASG will not terminate or launch additional instances. The cooldown happens after scaling activity and the reason for this behaviour is that some metrics have to be collected and stabilize before another scaling operating can take place.

exercises.question: Explain the default ASG termination policy
exercises.answer:
1. It finds the AZ which the most number of EC2 instances
2. If number of instances > 1, choose the one with oldest launch configuration, template and terminate it

exercises.question: True or False? by default, ASG tries to balance the number of instances across AZ
exercises.answer: True, this is why when it terminates instances, it chooses the AZ with the most instances.

exercises.question: Explain Lifecycle hooks in regards to Auto Scaling Groups
exercises.answer: Lifecycle hooks allows you perform extra steps before the instance goes in service (During pending state) or before it terminates (during terminating state).

exercises.question: If you use ASG and you would like to run extra steps before the instance goes in service, what will you use?
exercises.answer: Lifecycle hooks in pending state.

exercises.question: Describe one way to test ASG actually works
exercises.answer:
In Linux instances, you can install the 'stress' package and run stress to load the system for certain period of time and see if ASG kicks in by adding additional capacity (= more instances).

For example: `sudo stress --cpu 100 --timeout 20`

exercises.question: What is the shared responsibility model? What AWS is responsible for and what the user is responsible for based on the shared responsibility model?
exercises.answer:
The shared responsibility model defines what the customer is responsible for and what AWS is responsible for.

More on the shared responsibility model [here](https://aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model)

exercises.question: True or False? Based on the shared responsibility model, Amazon is responsible for physical CPUs and security groups on instances
exercises.answer: False. It is responsible for Hardware in its sites but not for security groups which created and managed by the users.

exercises.question: Explain "Shared Controls" in regards to the shared responsibility model
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "apply to both the infrastructure layer and customer layers, but in completely separate contexts or perspectives. In a shared control, AWS provides the requirements for the infrastructure and the customer must provide their own control implementation within their use of AWS services"

Learn more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model)

exercises.question: How to secure instances in AWS?
exercises.answer:
* Instance IAM roles should have minimal permissions needed. You don't want an instance-level incident to become an account-level incident
  * Use "AWS System Manager Session Manager" for SSH
  * Using latest OS images with your instances

exercises.question: What is AWS Artifact?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS Artifact is your go-to, central resource for compliance-related information that matters to you. It provides on-demand access to AWS’ security and compliance reports and select online agreements."

Read more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/artifact)

exercises.question: What is AWS Inspector?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon Inspector is an automated security assessment service that helps improve the security and compliance of applications deployed on AWS. Amazon Inspector automatically assesses applications for exposure, vulnerabilities, and deviations from best practices.""

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/inspector)

exercises.question: What is AWS Guarduty?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior to protect your Amazon Web Services accounts, workloads, and data stored in Amazon S3" 
Monitor VPC Flow lows, DNS logs, CloudTrail S3 events and CloudTrail Mgmt events.

exercises.question: What is AWS Shield?
exercises.answer: AWS definition: "AWS Shield is a managed Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection service that safeguards applications running on AWS."

exercises.question: What is AWS CloudHSM?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS CloudHSM is a cloud-based hardware security module (HSM) that enables you to easily generate and use your own encryption keys on the AWS Cloud."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudhsm)

exercises.question: True or False? AWS Inspector can perform both network and host assessments
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: What is AWS Key Management Service (KMS)?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "KMS makes it easy for you to create and manage cryptographic keys and control their use across a wide range of AWS services and in your applications."
More on KMS [here](https://aws.amazon.com/kms)

exercises.question: What is AWS Acceptable Use Policy?
exercises.answer:
It describes prohibited uses of the web services offered by AWS.
More on AWS Acceptable Use Policy [here](https://aws.amazon.com/aup)

exercises.question: True or False? A user is not allowed to perform penetration testing on any of the AWS services
exercises.answer: False. On some services, like EC2, CloudFront and RDS, penetration testing is allowed.

exercises.question: True or False? DDoS attack is an example of allowed penetration testing activity
exercises.answer: False.

exercises.question: True or False? AWS Access Key is a type of MFA device used for AWS resources protection
exercises.answer: False. Security key is an example of an MFA device.

exercises.question: What is Amazon Cognito?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon Cognito handles user authentication and authorization for your web and mobile apps."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/index.html)

exercises.question: What is AWS ACM?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Certificate Manager is a service that lets you easily provision, manage, and deploy public and private Secure Sockets Layer/Transport Layer Security (SSL/TLS) certificates for use with AWS services and your internal connected resources."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/certificate-manager)

exercises.question: What is AWS RDS?
exercises.answer:
* Relational Database Service
* Managed DB service (you can't ssh the machine)
* Supports multiple DBs: MySQL, Oracle, Aurora (AWS Proprietary), ...

exercises.question: Why to use AWS RDS instead of launching an EC2 instance and install a database on it?
exercises.answer:
AWS RDS is a managed service, that means it's automatically provisioned and patched for you.

In addition, it provides you with continuous backup (and the ability to restore from any point of time), scaling capability (both horizontal and vertical), monitoring dashboard and read replicas.

exercises.question: What do you know about RDS backups?
exercises.answer:
* Automated backups
* Full daily backup (done during maintenance window)
* Transactions logs backup every 5 minutes
* Retention can be increased and by default it's 7 days

exercises.question: Explain AWS RDS Storage Auto Scaling
exercises.answer:
* RDS storage can automatically be increased upon lack in storage
* The user needs to set "Maximum Storage Threshold" to have some limit on storage scaling
* Use cases: applications with unpredictable workloads
* Supports multiple RDS database engines

exercises.question: Explain Amazon RDS Read Replicas
exercises.answer:
[AWS Docs](https://aws.amazon.com/rds/features/read-replicas): "Amazon RDS Read Replicas provide enhanced performance and durability for RDS database (DB) instances. They make it easy to elastically scale out beyond the capacity constraints of a single DB instance for read-heavy database workloads."

In simpler words, it allows you to scale your reads.

exercises.question: True or False? RDS read replicas are supported within az, cross az and cross region
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: True or False? RDS read replicas are asynchronous
exercises.answer: True. This is done so the reads are consistent.

exercises.question: True or False? Amazon RDS supports MongoDB
exercises.answer: False. RDS is relational database and MongoDB is a NoSQL db.

exercises.question: What are some use cases for using RDS read replicas?
exercises.answer: You have a main application which works against your database but you would like to add additional app, one used for logging, analytics, ... so you prefer it won't use the same database. In this case, you create a read replica instance and the second application works against that instance.

exercises.question: Explain RDS Multi Availability Zone
exercises.answer:
* RDS multi AZ used mainly for disaster recovery purposes
* There is an RDS master instance and in another AZ an RDS standby instance
* The data is synced synchronously between them
* The user, application is accessing one DNS name and where there is a failure with the master instance, the DNS name moves to the standby instance, so the failover done automatically

exercises.question: True or False? Moving AWS RDS from single AZ to multi AZ is an operation with downtime (meaning there is a need to stop the DB)
exercises.answer: False. It's a zero downtime operation = no need to stop the database.

exercises.question: How AWS RDS switches from single AZ to multi AZ?
exercises.answer:
1. Snapshot is taken by RDS
2. The snapshot is restored to another, standby, RDS instance
3. Synchronization is enabled between the two instances

exercises.question: True or False? RDS encryption should be defined at launch time
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: True or False? in regards to RDS, replicas can be encrypted even if the master isn't encrypted
exercises.answer: False

exercises.question: How to make RDS snapshots encrypted?
exercises.answer:
* If RDS database is encrypted then, the snapshot itself is also encrypted
* If RDS database isn't encrypted then, the snapshot itself isn't encrypted and then you can copy the un-encrypted snapshot to created an encrypted copy

exercises.question: How to encrypt an un-encrypted RDS instance?
exercises.answer: Create a copy of the un-encrypted instance -> copy the snapshot to create an encrypted copy -> restore the database from the encrypted snapshot -> migrate the application to work against the copied instance -> remove the original DB instance

exercises.question: How IAM authentication works with RDS?
exercises.answer:
For example:

1. EC2 instance uses IAM role to make an API call to get auth token
2. The token, with SSL encryption, is used for accessing the RDS instance

Note: The token has a lifetime of 15 minutes

exercises.question: True or False? In case of RDS (not Aurora), read replicas require you to change the SQL connection string
exercises.answer: True. Since read replicas add endpoints, each with its own DNS name, you need to modify your app to reference these new endpoints to balance the load read.

exercises.question: What do you know about Amazon Aurora?
exercises.answer:
* A MySQL & Postgresql based relational database.
* Proprietary technology from AWS
* The default database proposed for the user when using RDS for creating a database.
* Storage automatically grows in increments of 10 GiB
* HA native - failover in instant
* Has better performances over MySQL and Postgres
* Supports 15 replicas (while MySQL supports 5)

exercises.question: True or False? Aurora stores 4 copies of your data across 2 availability zones
exercises.answer: False. It stores 6 copies across 3 availability zones

exercises.question: True or False? Aurora support self healing where corrupted data replaced by doing peer-to-peer replication
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: True or False? Aurora storage is striped across 20 volumes
exercises.answer: False. 100 volumes.

exercises.question: True or False? It's possible to scale Aurora replicas
exercises.answer: True. If your read replica instances exhaust their CPU, you can scale by adding more instances

exercises.question: Explain Aurora Serverless. What use cases is it good for?
exercises.answer:
* Aurora serverless is an automated database instantiation and it's auto scaled based on an actual usage
* It's good mainly for infrequent or unpredictable workflows
* You pay per second so it can eventually be more cost effective

exercises.question: What is the use case for Aurora multi-master?
exercises.answer: Aurora multi-master is perfect for a use case where you want to have instant failover for write node.

exercises.question: Explain "Point-in-Time Recovery" feature in DynamoDB
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "You can create on-demand backups of your Amazon DynamoDB tables, or you can enable continuous backups using point-in-time recovery. For more information about on-demand backups, see On-Demand Backup and Restore for DynamoDB."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/PointInTimeRecovery.html)

exercises.question: Explain "Global Tables" in DynamoDB
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "A global table is a collection of one or more replica tables, all owned by a single AWS account."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/V2globaltables_HowItWorks.html)

exercises.question: What is DynamoDB Accelerator?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is a fully managed, highly available, in-memory cache for DynamoDB that delivers up to a 10x performance improvement – from milliseconds to microseconds..."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/dynamodb/dax)

exercises.question: What is AWS ElastiCache? In what use case should it be used?
exercises.answer:
Amazon Elasticache is a fully managed Redis or Memcached in-memory data store.
It's great for read-intensive workloads where the common data/queries are cached and apps/users access the cache instead of the primary database.

exercises.question: Describe the workflow of an application using the cache in AWS
exercises.answer:
1. The application performs a query against the DB. There is a check to see if the data is in the cache
  1. If it is, it's a "cache hit" and the data is retrieved from there
  2. If it's not in there, it's a "cache miss" and the data is pulled from the database
   1. The data is then also written to the cache (assuming it is often accessed) and next time the user queries for the same data, it might be retrieved from the cache (depends on how much time passed and whether this specific data was i...

exercises.question: How can you make an application stateless using ElastiCache?
exercises.answer:
Let's say you have multiple instances running the same application and every time you use the application, it creates a user session.
This user session can be stored in ElastiCache so even if the user contacts a different instance of the application, the application can retrieve the session from the ElsatiCache.

exercises.question: You need a highly available cache with backup and restore features. Which one would you use?
exercises.answer: ElastiCache Redis.

exercises.question: You need a cache with read replicas that can be scaled and one support multi AZ. Which one would you use?
exercises.answer: ElastiCache Redis.

exercises.question: You need a cache that supports sharding and built with multi-threaded architecture in mind. Which one would you use?
exercises.answer: ElastiCache Memcached

exercises.question: True or False? ElastiCache doesn't supports IAM authentication
exercises.answer: True.

exercises.question: What patterns are there for loading data into the cache?
exercises.answer:
* Write Through: add or update data in the cache when the data is written to the DB
* Lazy Loading: all the read data is cached
* Session Store: store temporary session data in cache

exercises.question: What is AWS Redshift and how is it different than RDS?
exercises.answer: cloud data warehouse

exercises.question: What do you if you suspect AWS Redshift performs slowly?
exercises.answer:
* You can confirm your suspicion by going to AWS Redshift console and see running queries graph. This should tell you if there are any long-running queries.
* If confirmed, you can query for running queries and cancel the irrelevant queries
* Check for connection leaks (query for running connections and include their IP)
* Check for table locks and kill irrelevant locking sessions

exercises.question: What is Amazon DocumentDB?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility) is a fast, scalable, highly available, and fully managed document database service that supports MongoDB workloads. As a document database, Amazon DocumentDB makes it easy to store, query, and index JSON data."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/documentdb)

exercises.question: What type of storage is used by Amazon RDS?
exercises.answer: EBS

exercises.question: What would you use for automating code/software deployments?
exercises.answer: AWS CodeDeploy

exercises.question: You would like to invoke a function every time you enter a URL in the browser. Which service would you use for that?
exercises.answer: AWS Lambda

exercises.question: What would you use for easily creating similar AWS environments/resources for different customers?
exercises.answer: CloudFormation

exercises.question: Using which service, can you add user sign-up, sign-in and access control to mobile and web apps?
exercises.answer: Cognito

exercises.question: Which service would you use for building a website or web application?
exercises.answer: Lightsail

exercises.question: Which tool would you use for choosing between Reserved instances or On-Demand instances?
exercises.answer: Cost Explorer

exercises.question: What would you use to check how many unassociated Elastic IP address you have?
exercises.answer: Trusted Advisor

exercises.question: Which service allows you to transfer large amounts (Petabytes) of data in and out of the AWS cloud?
exercises.answer: AWS Snowball

exercises.question: Which service would you use if you need a data warehouse?
exercises.answer: AWS RedShift

exercises.question: Which service provides a virtual network dedicated to your AWS account?
exercises.answer: VPC

exercises.question: What you would use for having automated backups for an application that has MySQL database layer?
exercises.answer: Amazon Aurora

exercises.question: What would you use to migrate on-premise database to AWS?
exercises.answer: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS)

exercises.question: What would you use to check why certain EC2 instances were terminated?
exercises.answer: AWS CloudTrail

exercises.question: What would you use for SQL database?
exercises.answer: AWS RDS

exercises.question: What would you use for NoSQL database?
exercises.answer: AWS DynamoDB

exercises.question: What would you use for adding image and video analysis to your application?
exercises.answer: AWS Rekognition

exercises.question: Which service would you use for debugging and improving performances issues with your applications?
exercises.answer: AWS X-Ray

exercises.question: Which service is used for sending notifications?
exercises.answer: SNS

exercises.question: What would you use for running SQL queries interactively on S3?
exercises.answer: AWS Athena

exercises.question: What would you use for preparing and combining data for analytics or ML?
exercises.answer: AWS Glue

exercises.question: Which service would you use for monitoring malicious activity and unauthorized behavior in regards to AWS accounts and workloads?
exercises.answer: Amazon GuardDuty

exercises.question: Which service would you use for centrally manage billing, control access, compliance, and security across multiple AWS accounts?
exercises.answer: AWS Organizations

exercises.question: Which service would you use for web application protection?
exercises.answer: AWS WAF

exercises.question: You would like to monitor some of your resources in the different services. Which service would you use for that?
exercises.answer: CloudWatch

exercises.question: Which service would you use for performing security assessment?
exercises.answer: AWS Inspector

exercises.question: Which service would you use for creating DNS record?
exercises.answer: Route 53

exercises.question: What would you use if you need a fully managed document database?
exercises.answer: Amazon DocumentDB

exercises.question: Which service would you use to add access control (or sign-up, sign-in forms) to your web/mobile apps?
exercises.answer: AWS Cognito

exercises.question: Which service is often referred to as "used for decoupling applications"?
exercises.answer: AWS SQS. Since it's a messaging queue so it allows applications to switch from synchronous communication to asynchronous one.

exercises.question: Which service would you use if you need messaging queue?
exercises.answer: Simple Queue Service (SQS)

exercises.question: Which service would you use if you need managed DDOS protection?
exercises.answer: AWS Shield

exercises.question: Which service would you use if you need store frequently used data for low latency access?
exercises.answer: ElastiCache

exercises.question: What would you use to transfer files over long distances between a client and an S3 bucket?
exercises.answer: Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration

exercises.question: Which services are involved in getting a custom string (based on the input) when inserting a URL in the browser?
exercises.answer:
Lambda - to define a function that gets an input and returns a certain string
API Gateway - to define the URL trigger (= when you insert the URL, the function is invoked).

exercises.question: Which service would you use for data or events streaming?
exercises.answer: Kinesis

exercises.question: Which (free) tool would you use to get information on cost savings?
exercises.answer: Trusted Advisor

exercises.question: You would like to have on-perm storage access to AWS storage. What would you use for that?
exercises.answer: Storage Gateway

exercises.question: What is Route 53?
exercises.answer:
[AWS Route 53](https://aws.amazon.com/route53): "Amazon Route 53 is a highly available and scalable cloud Domain Name System (DNS) web service..."

Some of Route 53 features:
  * Register domains
  * DNS service - domain name translations
  * Health checks - verify your app is available
  * Not a feature but its SLA is 100% availability

exercises.question: What it means that "Route 53 is an Authoritative DNS"?
exercises.answer: The customer can update DNS records

exercises.question: What each Route 53 record contains?
exercises.answer:
* Domain/subdomain name (e.g. blipblop.com)
* Value (e.g. 201.7.202.2)
* Record type (e.g. A, AAAA, MX)
* TTL: amount of time the  record is going to be cached
* Routing Policy: how to respond to queries

exercises.question: What DNS record types does Route 53 supports?
exercises.answer:
* A
* AAAA
* CNAME
* NS
* DS
* CAA
* SOA
* MX
* TXT
* SPF
* SRV
* NAPTR
* PTR

exercises.question: What are hosted zones?
exercises.answer: A container that includes records for defining how to route traffic from a domain and its subdomains

exercises.question: What types of hosted zones are there?
exercises.answer:
* Public Hosted Zones - include records to specify how to route traffic on the internet
* Private Hosted Zones - contain records that specify how you traffic within VPC(s)

exercises.question: What is the difference between CNAME record and an Alias record?
exercises.answer:
CNAME is used for mapping one hostname to any other hostname while Alias is used to map an hostname to an AWS resource.

In addition, Alias work for both root domain (somedomain.com) and non-root domain, while CNAME works only with non-root domain (foo.somedomain.com)

exercises.question: True or False? Alias record can be set up for an EC2 DNS name
exercises.answer: False

exercises.question: True or False? Alias record can be set up for an VPC interface endpoint
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: True or False? Alias record is only of type A or AAAA
exercises.answer: True

exercises.question: What is a routing policy in regards to AWS Route 53?
exercises.answer: A routing policy routing defines how Route 53 responds to DNS queries.

exercises.question: What Route 53 routing policies are there?
exercises.answer:
* Simple 
* Geolocation
* Failover
* Latency based
* Geoproximity
* Multi-Value Answer
* Weighted

exercises.question: Suppose you need to route % of your traffic to a certain instance and the rest of the traffic, to another instance. Which routing policy would you choose?
exercises.answer: Weighted routing policy.

exercises.question: Suppose you need to route traffic to a single source with Route 53, without any other requirements, which routing policy would you choose?
exercises.answer: The `simple` routing policy

exercises.question: Explain the geolocation routing policy
exercises.answer:
* Routing based on user location
* Location can be specified by continent, country or US state
* It's recommended to have a default record in case there is no match on location

exercises.question: What are some use cases for using geolocation routing policy?
exercises.answer:
* Restrict content distribution
* App localization
* Load balancing

exercises.question: Explain the geoproximity routing policy
exercises.answer:
* Route based on the geographic location of resources
* Shifting routing is done based on the `bias` value
* Resources can be of AWS and non-AWS type
    * For non-AWS you have to specify latitude and longitude in addition to AWS region as done in AWS-based resources
* To use it, you have to use Route 53 traffic flow

exercises.question: What are some use cases for weighted routing policy?
exercises.answer:
* Load balancing between regions
* Testing new applications versions

exercises.question: True or False? Route 53 simple routing policy supports both single and multiple values
exercises.answer:
True.

If multiple values are returned from Route 53 then, the client chooses a single value to use.

exercises.question: True or False? In weighted routing DNS records must have the same name but not the same type
exercises.answer: False. They must have the same name AND type.

exercises.question: You would like to use a routing policy that will take latency into account and will route to the resource with the lowest latency. Which routing policy would you use?
exercises.answer: Latency-based routing policy.

exercises.question: What happens when you set all records to weight 0 when using Weighted routing policy?
exercises.answer: All records are used equally.

exercises.question: What Route 53 health checks are used for?
exercises.answer:
Automated DNS failover based on monitoring:

  * Another health check
  * endpoint (app, AWS resource, server)
  * CloudWatch alarms

exercises.question: You would like to use a routing policy based on the resource location and be able to shift more traffic to some resources. Which one would you use?
exercises.answer: Geoproximity routing policy

exercises.question: Explain Route 53 Traffic Flow feature
exercises.answer:
It's a visual editor for managing complex routing decision trees. It allows you to simplify the process of managing records.

Configuration can be saved (as Traffic Flow Policy) and applied to different domains/hosted zones. In addition, it supports versioning

exercises.question: What are calculated health checks?
exercises.answer: When you combine the results of multiple health checks into a single health check.

exercises.question: What is one possible use case for using calculated health checks?
exercises.answer: Performing maintenance for a website without causing all the health checks to fail.

exercises.question: You would like to use a routing policy based on the user location. Which one would you use?
exercises.answer:
Geolocation routing policy. It's based on user location.

Don't confuse it with latency-based routing policy. While shorter distance may result in lower latency, this is not the requirement in the question.

exercises.question: True or False? Route 53 Multi Value is a substitute for those who want cheaper solution than ELB
exercises.answer: False. Route 53 Multi Value is not a substitute for ELB. It's focused on client-side load balancing as opposed to ELB.

exercises.question: True or False? Domain registrar and DNS service is inherently the same thing
exercises.answer: False. DNS service can be Route 53 (where you manage DNS records) while the domain itself can be purchased from other sources that aren't Amazon related (e.g. GoDadday).

exercises.question: What is Simple Queue Service (SQS)?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon Simple Queue Service (SQS) is a fully managed message queuing service that enables you to decouple and scale microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications".

Learn more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/sqs)

exercises.question: Explain "producer" and "consumer" in regards to messaging queue
exercises.answer:
Producer is the application or in general, the source that sends messages to the queue.

Consumer is the process or application that pulls the messages from the queue.

exercises.question: What "default retention of messages" means?
exercises.answer:
It refers to a retention period in which a message has to consumed/processed and deleted from the queue.

As of today, the retention of a message is 4 days by default and the maximum allows is 14 days.

exercises.question:
What's the limitation on message size in SQS?

* 128KB
* 128MB
* 256KB
* 256MB
exercises.answer: 256KB

exercises.question: True or False? It's possible to have duplicated messages in the queue
exercises.answer: True. It's referred to as "at least once delivery".

exercises.question: True or False? "Consumers" can be only EC2 instances
exercises.answer: False. They can be Lambda functions and even on-premise instances

exercises.question: True or False? Processes/Applications use from the SDK the SendMessage API in order to send messages to the queue
exercises.answer: True.

exercises.question: What it means "best effort ordering" in regards to SQS?
exercises.answer: It means messages in the queue can be out of order.

exercises.question: What is "Delay Queue" in regards to SQS?
exercises.answer:
It's the time in seconds to delay the delivery of new messages (when they reached the queue already).

The limit as of today is 15 minutes.

exercises.question: What is "Visibility Timeout?"
exercises.answer:
The time in seconds for a message to not be visible for consumers.

The limit as of today is 12 hours

exercises.question: Give an example of architecture or workflow that involves SQS and EC2 & S3
exercises.answer:
A website that allows users to upload videos and adds subtitles to them:

1. First the user uploads the video through the web interface which uploads it to an S3 bucket
2. SQS gets notified with a message on the video location
3. EC2 instance (or Lambda function) starts to work on adding the subtitles
4. The video with the subtitles is uploaded to an S3 buckets
5. SQS gets notified of the result and specifically the video location

exercises.question: What is Simply Notification Service?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "a highly available, durable, secure, fully managed pub/sub messaging service that enables you to decouple microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications."

Read more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/sns)

exercises.question:
Explain the following in regards to SNS:

  - Topics
  - Subscribers
  - Publishers
exercises.answer:
* Topics - used for grouping multiple endpoints
  * Subscribers - the endpoints where topics send messages to
  * Publishers - the provider of the message (event, person, ...)

exercises.question: How SNS is different from SQS?
exercises.answer:
SNS, as opposed to SQS, works in a publisher/subscriber model. Where's SQS works in  Producer/Consumer model.

SQS delivers the message to one consumer where's SNS will send a message to multiple subscribers.

exercises.question: What's a Fan-Out pattern?
exercises.answer: A messaging pattern where a single message is send to multiple destinations (often simultaneously). So one-to-many broadcast message.

exercises.question: What is AWS CloudWatch?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon CloudWatch is a monitoring and observability service..."

More on CloudWatch [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch)

exercises.question: What is AWS CloudTrail?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS CloudTrail is a service that enables governance, compliance, operational auditing, and risk auditing of your AWS account."

Read more on CloudTrail [here](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudtrail)

exercises.question: What are Service Control Policies and to what service they belong?
exercises.answer:
AWS organizations service and the definition by Amazon: "SCPs offer central control over the maximum available permissions for all accounts in your organization, allowing you to ensure your accounts stay within your organization’s access control guidelines."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scp.html)

exercises.question: Explain AWS pricing model
exercises.answer:
It mainly works on "pay-as-you-go" meaning you pay only for what are using and when you are using it.
In s3 you pay for 1. How much data you are storing 2. Making requests (PUT, POST, ...)
In EC2 it's based on the purchasing option (on-demand, spot, ...), instance type, AMI type and the region used.

More on AWS pricing model [here](https://aws.amazon.com/pricing)

exercises.question: How do you estimate AWS costs?
exercises.answer:
* TCO calculator
* AWS simple calculator
* Cost Explorer
* AWS Budgets
* Cost Allocation Tags

exercises.question: What basic support in AWS includes?
exercises.answer:
* 24x7 customer service
* Trusted Advisor
* AWS personal Health Dashoard

exercises.question: What is Amazon Connect?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon Connect is an easy to use omnichannel cloud contact center that helps companies provide superior customer service at a lower cost."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/connect)

exercises.question: What are "APN Consulting Partners"?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "APN Consulting Partners are professional services firms that help customers of all types and sizes design, architect, build, migrate, and manage their workloads and applications on AWS, accelerating their journey to the cloud."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/partners/consulting)

exercises.question:
Which of the following are AWS accounts types (and are sorted by order)?

  - Basic, Developer, Business, Enterprise
  - Newbie, Intermediate, Pro, Enterprise
  - Developer, Basic, Business, Enterprise
  - Beginner, Pro, Intermediate Enterprise
exercises.answer: - Basic, Developer, Business, Enterprise

exercises.question: True or False? Region is a factor when it comes to EC2 costs/pricing
exercises.answer: True. You pay differently based on the chosen region.

exercises.question: What is "AWS Infrastructure Event Management"?
exercises.answer: AWS Definition: "AWS Infrastructure Event Management is a structured program available to Enterprise Support customers (and Business Support customers for an additional fee) that helps you plan for large-scale events such as product or application launches, infrastructure migrations, and marketing events."

exercises.question: What is "AWS Organizations"?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS Organizations helps you centrally govern your environment as you grow and scale your workloads on AWS."

Read more on Organizations [here](https://aws.amazon.com/organizations)

exercises.question: What's an OU in regards to AWS Organizations?'
exercises.answer:
OU (Organizational Units) is a way to group multiple accounts together so you can treat them as a single unit.

By default there is the "Root" OU created in AWS Organizations.

Most of the time OUs are based on functions or common set of controls.

exercises.question: What is AWS CodeDeploy?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS CodeDeploy is a fully managed deployment service that automates software deployments to a variety of compute services such as Amazon EC2, AWS Fargate, AWS Lambda, and your on-premises servers."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/codedeploy)

exercises.question: Explain what is CloudFormation
exercises.answer: AWS definition: "AWS CloudFormation is a service that helps you model and set up your Amazon Web Services resources so that you can spend less time managing those resources and more time focusing on your applications that run in AWS. You create a template that describes all the AWS resources that you want (like Amazon EC2 instances or Amazon RDS DB instances), and CloudFormation takes care of provisioning and configuring those resources for you."

exercises.question: What is AWS CDK?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "The AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK) is an open-source software development framework to define cloud infrastructure as code and provision it through AWS CloudFormation. CDK gives the flexibility to use popular programming languages like TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Java, C# and Go (in Developer Preview) to define your infrastructure, and AWS CDK provides a set of libraries for AWS services that abstract away the need to write raw CloudFormation templates.

Learn more [her...

exercises.question: What is AWS Lightsail?
exercises.answer: AWS definition: "Lightsail is an easy-to-use cloud platform that offers you everything needed to build an application or website, plus a cost-effective, monthly plan."

exercises.question: What is AWS Rekognition?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "Amazon Rekognition makes it easy to add image and video analysis to your applications using proven, highly scalable, deep learning technology that requires no machine learning expertise to use."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/rekognition)

exercises.question: What AWS Resource Groups used for?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "You can use resource groups to organize your AWS resources. Resource groups make it easier to manage and automate tasks on large numbers of resources at one time. "

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ARG/latest/userguide/welcome.html)

exercises.question: What is AWS Global Accelerator?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Global Accelerator is a service that improves the availability and performance of your applications with local or global users..."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/global-accelerator)

exercises.question: What is AWS Config?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS Config is a service that enables you to assess, audit, and evaluate the configurations of your AWS resources."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/config)

exercises.question: What is AWS X-Ray?
exercises.answer:
AWS definition: "AWS X-Ray helps developers analyze and debug production, distributed applications, such as those built using a microservices architecture."
Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/xray)

exercises.question: What is AWS OpsWorks?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "AWS OpsWorks is a configuration management service that provides managed instances of Chef and Puppet."

Learn more about it [here](https://aws.amazon.com/opsworks)

exercises.question: What is AWS Snowmobile?
exercises.answer:
"AWS Snowmobile is an Exabyte-scale data transfer service used to move extremely large amounts of data to AWS."

Learn more [here](https://aws.amazon.com/snowmobile)

exercises.question: What is AWS Athena?
exercises.answer:
"Amazon Athena is an interactive query service that makes it easy to analyze data in Amazon S3 using standard SQL."

Learn more about AWS Athena [here](https://aws.amazon.com/athena)

exercises.question: What is Amazon Cloud Directory?
exercises.answer:
Amazon definition: "Amazon Cloud Directory is a highly available multi-tenant directory-based store in AWS. These directories scale automatically to hundreds of millions of objects as needed for applications."

Learn more [here](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/clouddirectory/latest/developerguide/what_is_cloud_directory.html)

exercises.question: What is AWS Elastic Beanstalk?
exercises.answer:
